|birth=[[September 15]], [[1920s#1922|1922]]<ref name="Bob Anderson Death 1">{{WebCite|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/local/obituaries/bob-anderson-fencing-master-and-coach-to-hollywood-actors-dies-at-89/2012/01/02/gIQAmrmyWP_story.html|text=Bob Anderson, fencing master and coach to Hollywood actors, dies at 89|author=Shapiro, T.Rees|date=2012-01-02|work=[https://www.washingtonpost.com/ Washington Post]|archivedate=20120108105941}}</ref>

{{Quote|I never took up the sword; I think the sword took me up.|Bob Anderson|<ref name="Insider 132">{{InsiderCite|132|Launchpad|Launch Pad}}</ref>}}
'''Robert James Gilbert Anderson''', known as '''Bob Anderson''' ([[September 15]], [[1920s|1922]] – [[January 1]], [[2012]]), was the swordmaster who played [[Anakin Skywalker|Darth Vader]] in his various fight scenes in [[Star Wars: Episode V The Empire Strikes Back|''The Empire Strikes Back'']] and [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']]. He replaced [[David Prowse]] due to the actor's tendency to break [[lightsaber]]s. Because of the height differences—Anderson was 6'1" (1.85m), while Prowse was 6'7" (2.02m)—Anderson's scenes were filmed from a lower angle to make him seem taller, or he stood on some small stilts or wore platform shoes. Anderson also played [[Alliance to Restore the Republic|Rebel]] [[Officer]] [[Trey Callum]] during the [[Battle of Hoth]] in ''The Empire Strikes Back''. Anderson later did swordfighting choreography and training for films such films as the [[w:c:lotr:The Lord of the Rings film trilogy|''The Lord of the Rings'' trilogy]] (with actor [[Christopher Lee]]) and the James Bond film ''[[Wikipedia:Die Another Day|Die Another Day]]'' (with stunt performer [[Jim Dowdall]]).

==Biography==
Born in [[Wikipedia:Hampshire|Hampshire]], [[Wikipedia:England|England]], Bob Anderson joined the Royal Marines and won several combined services titles in the sport of fencing. He served in the [[Wikipedia:Mediterranean Sea|Mediterranean Sea]] during World War II. As a competitive fencer, he represented [[Wikipedia:Great Britain|Great Britain]] at the [[Wikipedia:Olympic Games|Olympic Games]] in 1952 and the World Championships in 1950 and 1953 in the sabre event. He finished tied for fifth in the team sabre event at [[Wikipedia:Helsinki|Helsinki]] in 1952.<ref name="Bob Anderson Death 1" />

Bob Anderson's cinema career began in 1952,<ref>{{WebCite|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/01/03/movies/bob-anderson-sword-fight-choreographer-dies-at-89.html|text=Bob Anderson, Sword-Fight Choreographer, Dies at 89|author=Weber, Bruce|date=2012-01-03|work=[htpps://www.nytimes.com/ The New York Times]|archivedate=20120129231122}}</ref> when he choreographed fights for and coached Errol Flynn for the 1953 film ''[[Wikipedia:The Master of Ballantrae (film)|The Master of Ballantrae]]''.<ref name="Bob Anderson Death 1" /><ref name="Bob Anderson Death 2">{{WebCite|url=http://www.cbsnews.com/8301-207_162-57350921/movie-sword-fight-master-bob-anderson-dies/|text="Movie sword-fight master Bob Anderson dies"|date=2012-01-02|work=[http://www.cbsnews.com/ CBS News]|archivedate=20120415181924}}</ref><ref name="Bob Anderson Death 3">{{WebCite|url=http://www.ontheredcarpet.com/Darth-Vader-sword-master-Bob-Anderson-dies-at-89/8490109|text='Darth Vader' sword master Bob Anderson dies at 89|author=Heller, Corinne|date=2012-01-04|work=[http://www.ontheredcarpet.com/index OnTheRedCarpet]|archivedate=20120621194601}}</ref> During rehearsal for a scene he accidentally slashed Flynn on his thigh, leading to notoriety in Hollywood as "the man who stabbed Errol Flynn".<ref name="Bob Anderson Death 1" /> He went on to work as a stunt performer and/or fight choreographer in films such as ''The Guns of Navarone'' and the Bond films ''From Russia With Love'' and ''Casino Royale''.<ref name="Bob Anderson Death 2" />
<ref name="Bob Anderson Death 3" /> His stature in Hollywood was cemented when he was selected by Stanley Kubrick in 1974 to act as the sword master for Barry Lyndon.
[[File:Mark Hamill vs. Bob Anderson ESB.jpg|thumb|right|300px|[[Mark Hamill]] and Bob Anderson on set in the carbon freezing chamber in ''The Empire Strikes Back''.]]
For the ''[[Star Wars]]'' franchise, Bob Anderson was the swordmaster who played [[Anakin Skywalker|Darth Vader]] in his various fight scenes in [[Star Wars: Episode V The Empire Strikes Back|''The Empire Strikes Back'']] and [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']].<ref name="Bob Anderson Death 1" /><ref name="Bob Anderson Death 2" /><ref name="Bob Anderson Death 3" /> Anderson also played [[Alliance to Restore the Republic|Rebel]] [[Officer]] [[Trey Callum]] during the [[Battle of Hoth]] in ''The Empire Strikes Back''.<ref name="Bob Anderson autograph 1">{{WebCite|url=http://www.let-the-wookie-win.com/index.php?showimage=344|text=My Bob Anderson Starwars Autograph|author=Oldbury, Dave Louis|date=2011-06-21|work=Star Wars Autograph Collecting|nobackup=1}}</ref><ref name="Bob Anderson autograph 2">{{WebCite|url=http://www.let-the-wookie-win.com/index.php?showimage=862|text=My Bob Anderson Starwars Autograph|author=Oldbury, Dave Louis|date=2011-08-27|work=Star Wars Autograph Collecting|nobackup=1}}</ref><ref name="Bob Anderson autograph 3">{{WebCite|url=http://www.let-the-wookie-win.com/index.php?showimage=863|text=My Bob Anderson Starwars Autograph|author=Oldbury, Dave Louis|date=2011-08-27|work=Star Wars Autograph Collecting|nobackup=1}}</ref>
[[File:Lukevaderunrotoscoped.jpg|thumb|left|300px|[[Mark Hamill]] and Bob Anderson on set fighting with carbon fiber rods in ''Return of the Jedi''.]]
Anderson did not receive much recognition for his role as doubling for Darth Vader in the ''Star Wars'' films for years after their initial release, in part because David Prowse was so lauded for his portrayal that director [[George Lucas]] did not want to detract from the boost it gave the actor's career. In a 1983 interview, however, [[Mark Hamill]] paid homage to Anderson's contribution, saying: ''"Bob Anderson was the man who actually did Vader's fighting. It was always supposed to be a secret, but I finally told George I didn't think it was fair any more. Bob worked so bloody hard that he deserves some recognition. It's ridiculous to preserve the myth that it's all done by one man."''<ref name="Bob Anderson Death 1" /><ref name="Bob Anderson Death 2" />

[[File:BobAnderson.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Swordmaster Bob Anderson.]]

For the next thirty years, Anderson continued to work in cinema and was responsible for the swordsmanship in many films. He had a reputation for being a perfectionist, with director Martin Campbell giving him the nickname "Grumpy Bob". Among his credits as a fight choreographer and fencing coach are ''[[Wikipedia:The Three Musketeers (1993 film)|The Three Musketeers]]'', ''[[Wikipedia:First Knight|First Knight]]'' with Sean Connery, ''[[Wikipedia:The Mask of Zorro|The Mask of Zorro]]'' with actor Antonio Banderas, [[Wikipedia:The Lord of the Rings film trilogy|''The Lord of the Rings'' trilogy]] with actor [[Christopher Lee]], the James Bond film ''[[Wikipedia:Die Another Day|Die Another Day]]'' with stunt performer [[Jim Dowdall]], and ''[[w:c:pirates:Pirates of the Caribbean: The Curse of the Black Pearl|Pirates of the Caribbean: The Curse of the Black Pearl]]'' with Johnny Depp. Shortly before his death, he was working on ''[[Wikipedia:The Hobbit (film series)|The Hobbit]]''.<ref name="Bob Anderson Death 1" /><ref name="Bob Anderson Death 2" /><ref name="Bob Anderson Death 3" />

Anderson died on New Year's Day in 2012. He was 89 years old.<ref name="Bob Anderson Death 1" /><ref name="Bob Anderson Death 2" /><ref name="Bob Anderson Death 3" />
